title: 3 Value Stocks We’re Skeptical Of
content:
Value stocks typically trade at discounts to the broader market, offering patient investors the opportunity to buy businesses when they’re out of favor. The key risk, however, is that these stocks are usually cheap for a reason – five cents for a piece of fruit may seem like a great deal until you find out it’s rotten.

This distinction between true value and value traps can challenge even the most skilled investors. Luckily for you, we started StockStory to help you uncover exceptional companies. That said, here are three value stocks with poor fundamentals and some alternatives you should consider instead.

Sensata Technologies (ST)

Forward P/E Ratio: 10.6x

Originally a temperature sensor control maker and a subsidiary of Texas Instruments for 60 years, Sensata Technology Holdings (NYSE: ST) is a leading supplier of analog sensors used in industrial and transportation applications, best known for its dominant position in the tire pressure monitoring systems in cars.

Why Should You Sell ST?

Customers postponed purchases of its products and services this cycle as its revenue declined by 4.4% annually over the last two years Demand will likely be soft over the next 12 months as Wall Street’s estimates imply tepid growth of 3% Gross margin of 29.3% reflects its high production costs

Sensata Technologies is trading at $38.92 per share, or 10.6x forward P/E. Read our free research report to see why you should think twice about including ST in your portfolio, it’s free.

Franklin Resources (BEN)

Forward P/E Ratio: 9.8x

Operating under the widely recognized Franklin Templeton brand since 1947, Franklin Resources (NYSE:BEN) is a global investment management organization that offers financial services and solutions to individuals, institutions, and wealth advisors worldwide.

Why Do We Avoid BEN?

Muted 4.5% annual revenue growth over the last two years shows its demand lagged behind its financials peers Earnings per share fell by 2.5% annually over the last five years while its revenue grew, showing its incremental sales were much less profitable Below-average return on equity indicates management struggled to find compelling investment opportunities

At $24.86 per share, Franklin Resources trades at 9.8x forward P/E. If you’re considering BEN for your portfolio, see our FREE research report to learn more.

Forestar Group (FOR)

Forward P/E Ratio: 8.9x

As a majority-owned subsidiary of homebuilding giant D.R. Horton, Forestar Group (NYSE:FOR) develops and sells finished residential lots to homebuilders, focusing primarily on land acquisition and development for single-family homes.

Story Continues

Why Do We Steer Clear of FOR?

11.2% annual revenue growth over the last five years was slower than its consumer discretionary peers Cash-burning history makes us doubt the long-term viability of its business model Eroding returns on capital from an already low base indicate that management’s recent investments are destroying value

Forestar Group’s stock price of $25.99 implies a valuation ratio of 8.9x forward P/E. Dive into our free research report to see why there are better opportunities than FOR.

title: Is It Too Late To Consider Sensata Technologies (ST) After Its 102% One Year Rally?
content:
Get insights on thousands of stocks from the global community of over 7 million individual investors at Simply Wall St.

If you are wondering whether Sensata Technologies Holding at US$38.69 is still offering value after its recent run, this breakdown will help you see what the current price actually reflects. The stock has recently shown strong momentum, with returns of 11.8% over 7 days, 15.4% over 30 days, 10.9% year to date, and 102.1% over 1 year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns of 14.5% decline and 30.1% decline tell a different story over longer periods. Recent market attention around Sensata has centered on how its share price recovery contrasts with the weaker multi year returns. This has prompted questions about whether sentiment has shifted or if the stock was previously priced too pessimistically. This mix of shorter term strength and longer term weakness is an important backdrop when you think about what the current valuation might be implying. On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Sensata Technologies Holding currently scores 3 out of 6. The rest of this article will walk through the main valuation approaches behind that score and then finish with a broader way to judge whether the market price truly fits the story.

Find out why Sensata Technologies Holding's 102.1% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: Sensata Technologies Holding Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash the business could generate for shareholders rather than just earnings multiples.

For Sensata Technologies Holding, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $471.2 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2030, including a projection of $583.5 million in that year, and Simply Wall St extrapolates the later years in the 10 year path using its own assumptions. All figures are in US$.

Bringing these projected cash flows back to today results in an estimated intrinsic value of $40.26 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $38.69, the DCF points to Sensata trading at about a 3.9% discount. This sits in the “roughly in line” zone rather than suggesting a large gap either way.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Sensata Technologies Holding is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Story Continues

ST Discounted Cash Flow as at Apr 2026

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Sensata Technologies Holding.

Approach 2: Sensata Technologies Holding Price vs Sales

For companies that are generating revenue but may not have earnings that line up cleanly with short term market moves, the P/S ratio is a useful way to compare what investors are paying for each dollar of sales.

Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk tend to justify a higher “normal” P/S multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one. That is why simple comparisons can sometimes be misleading.

At the moment, Sensata Technologies Holding trades on a P/S of 1.52x. This compares with an Electrical industry average P/S of 2.33x and a peer group average of 7.14x, so the stock is priced below both of those benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Sensata on a P/S basis is 1.49x. This Fair Ratio reflects a tailored view of what the multiple could be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, market cap, industry and company specific risks, rather than relying only on broad industry or peer averages.

Because this Fair Ratio is designed to incorporate these company specific features, it can offer a more rounded reference point than a straight comparison with peers. With the current P/S of 1.52x sitting very close to the Fair Ratio of 1.49x, the shares look priced roughly in line with those fundamentals.

Result: ABOUT RIGHTNYSE:ST P/S Ratio as at Apr 2026

For Sensata, one investor might build a more cautious Narrative anchored around a fair value of US$34.0 per share with assumptions such as 2.6% annual revenue growth and profit margins at 11.6%. Another investor could prefer a more optimistic Narrative closer to US$48.0 per share, using higher revenue growth and margin assumptions. title: Why Is Sensata Technologies (ST) Stock Rocketing Higher Today
content:
What Happened?

Shares of sensor manufacturer Sensata Technology (NYSE:ST) jumped 7.1% in the afternoon session as semiconductor stocks rallied in response to the de-escalation of the U.S.-Iran conflict.

The sector rallied specifically because semiconductors were highly vulnerable to the supply chain disruptions that occurred during the war. The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is a critical victory for the industry, as the waterway is essential for the transit of noble gases and materials used in chip fabrication.

Is now the time to buy Sensata Technologies? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.

What Is The Market Telling Us

Sensata Technologies’s shares are quite volatile and have had 17 moves greater than 5% over the last year. In that context, today’s move indicates the market considers this news meaningful but not something that would fundamentally change its perception of the business.

The previous big move we wrote about was 15 days ago when the stock gained 4.9% on the news that the Trump administration announced a plan to create a voluntary investment consortium targeting $4 trillion in funding for semiconductor supply chains, energy projects, and critical minerals.

The initiative, an expansion of the 'Pax Silica' program launched in December 2025, aims to secure access to AI and chipmaking for the U.S. and its allies. According to reports, the U.S. government will contribute an initial $250 million toward the ambitious goal. This move signals strong government support for the domestic and allied chip industry, boosting investor confidence.

The prospect of significant capital infusion into the sector could lead to the development of new manufacturing facilities, research advancements, and a more resilient global supply chain, benefiting chipmakers and related technology companies.

Sensata Technologies is up 7.3% since the beginning of the year, and at $37.45 per share, it is trading close to its 52-week high of $38.36 from February 2026. Despite the year-to-date gain, investors who bought $1,000 worth of Sensata Technologies’s shares 5 years ago would now be looking at only $646.47.

title: Q4 Earnings Highs And Lows: onsemi (NASDAQ:ON) Vs The Rest Of The Analog Semiconductors Stocks
content:
Wrapping up Q4 earnings, we look at the numbers and key takeaways for the analog semiconductors stocks, including onsemi (NASDAQ:ON) and its peers.

Demand for analog chips is generally linked to the overall level of economic growth, as analog chips serve as the building blocks of most electronic goods and equipment. Unlike digital chip designers, analog chip makers tend to produce the majority of their own chips, as analog chip production does not require expensive leading edge nodes. Less dependent on major secular growth drivers, analog product cycles are much longer, often 5-7 years.

The 15 analog semiconductors stocks we track reported a satisfactory Q4. As a group, revenues beat analysts’ consensus estimates by 0.7% while next quarter’s revenue guidance was above.

While some analog semiconductors stocks have fared somewhat better than others, they have collectively declined. On average, share prices are down 4.7% since the latest earnings results.

onsemi (NASDAQ:ON)

Spun out of Motorola in 1999 and built through a series of acquisitions, onsemi (NASDAQ:ON) is a global provider of analog chips specializing in autos, industrial applications, and power management in cloud data centers.

onsemi reported revenues of $1.53 billion, down 11.2% year on year. This print was in line with analysts’ expectations, but overall, it was a slower quarter for the company with revenue guidance for next quarter slightly missing analysts’ expectations and revenue in line with analysts’ estimates.

“We remained disciplined in our execution and met expectations in the fourth quarter as we saw increasing signs of stabilization in our key markets,” said Hassane El-Khoury, President and CEO, onsemi.onsemi Total Revenue

Unsurprisingly, the stock is down 2.6% since reporting and currently trades at $63.40.

Read our full report on onsemi here, it’s free.

Best Q4: Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ:SWKS)

Result of a merger of Alpha Industries and the wireless communications division of Conexant, Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ: SWKS) is a designer and manufacturer of chips used in smartphones, autos, and industrial applications to amplify, filter, and process wireless signals.

Skyworks Solutions reported revenues of $1.04 billion, down 3.1% year on year, outperforming analysts’ expectations by 3.4%. The business had an exceptional quarter with a beat of analysts’ EPS and adjusted operating income estimates.Skyworks Solutions Total Revenue

Skyworks Solutions achieved the biggest analyst estimates beat among its peers. Although it had a fine quarter compared to its peers, the market seems unhappy with the results as the stock is down 1.7% since reporting. It currently trades at $55.

Story Continues

Is now the time to buy Skyworks Solutions? Access our full analysis of the earnings results here, it’s free.

Weakest Q4: Vishay Intertechnology (NYSE:VSH)

Named after the founder's ancestral village in present-day Lithuania, Vishay Intertechnology (NYSE:VSH) manufactures simple chips and electronic components that are building blocks of virtually all types of electronic devices.

Vishay Intertechnology reported revenues of $800.9 million, up 12.1% year on year, exceeding analysts’ expectations by 0.7%. Still, it was a slower quarter as it posted a significant miss of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates and EPS in line with analysts’ estimates.

As expected, the stock is down 9.5% since the results and currently trades at $18.77.

Read our full analysis of Vishay Intertechnology’s results here.

Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN)

Headquartered in Dallas, Texas since the 1950s, Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN) is the world’s largest producer of analog semiconductors.

Texas Instruments reported revenues of $4.42 billion, up 10.4% year on year. This print lagged analysts' expectations by 0.8%. It was a slower quarter as it also logged a significant miss of analysts’ EPS and revenue estimates.

Texas Instruments had the weakest performance against analyst estimates among its peers. The stock is up 1.3% since reporting and currently trades at $199.21.

Read our full, actionable report on Texas Instruments here, it’s free.

Sensata Technologies (NYSE:ST)

Originally a temperature sensor control maker and a subsidiary of Texas Instruments for 60 years, Sensata Technology Holdings (NYSE: ST) is a leading supplier of analog sensors used in industrial and transportation applications, best known for its dominant position in the tire pressure monitoring systems in cars.

Sensata Technologies reported revenues of $917.9 million, up 1.1% year on year. This number topped analysts’ expectations by 0.6%. Zooming out, it was a mixed quarter as it also produced a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates but revenue guidance for next quarter meeting analysts’ expectations.

The stock is down 3.8% since reporting and currently trades at $34.79.

Read our full, actionable report on Sensata Technologies here, it’s free.

Market Update

Late in 2025 into early 2026, there was hand wringing around artificial intelligence. For software companies, the fear was that AI would erode pricing power and compress margins as new tools made it easier to replicate what once required expensive enterprise platforms. Crypto investors had their own version of the same anxiety: if AI agents could trade, allocate capital, and manage wallets autonomously, what exactly was the long-term value of today’s crypto infrastructure?

These concerns triggered a noticeable rotation away from these sectors and into safer havens. But markets rarely dwell on one narrative for long. Spring 2026 came, and the focus shifted abruptly from technological disruption to geopolitical risk. The US’ conflict with Iran became the dominant driver of market psychology, and when geopolitics takes center stage, the script changes quickly. Investors stop debating growth rates and start worrying about oil supply, inflation, and global stability.

title: Zacks Industry Outlook Highlights Sensata Technologies and Thermon
content:
For Immediate Release

Chicago, IL – March 27, 2026 – Today, Zacks Equity Research discusses Sensata Technologies Holding plc ST and Thermon Group Holdings, Inc. THR.

Industry: Instruments - Control

Link: https://www.zacks.com/commentary/2890117/2-instruments-stocks-set-to-ride-on-energy-efficient-industrial-thrust

The Zacks Instruments – Control industry is expected to benefit from energy-efficient production processes and integrated software systems. The rising demand for state-of-the-art technology to replace legacy industrial controls with automated products amid the worldwide energy crisis is likely to support growth.

However, elevated customer inventory levels, high infrastructure upgrade costs, price volatility and inflationary pressures may hinder the process automation and instrumentation market. Amid this backdrop, Sensata Technologies Holdingplc and Thermon Group Holdings, Inc. are likely to benefit from greater emphasis on energy efficiency, cost-cutting measures and a broad-based thrust on industrial automation and optimal resource utilization.

Industry Description

The Zacks Instruments - Control industry comprises manufacturers of precision and specialty motion-control components and systems used in a wide range of industries. These companies deliver sophisticated flow measurement, control and communication solutions for air, water and other forms of gas and liquid used for commercial and residential purposes.

The companies offer an array of products for fuel, combustion, fluid, actuation, electronic applications, energy control and optimization, particularly for the process industry. Some industry players offer heating, ventilation and air conditioning products. These include water heaters and electric heating systems for under-floor radiant applications for boiler manufacturers and alternative energy control packages. A few firms provide water reuse products, including drainage and rainwater harvesting solutions.

What's Shaping the Future of Instruments - Control Industry?

Greater Emphasis on Industrial Automation: A greater focus on increased adoption of automation across all industry verticals and higher investments in new technologies are expected to drive growth over the next few years, with North America leading the way. Rising infrastructural investments in the energy and power sector, increasing demand for organic food and nutritional beverages and favorable government policies are aiding growth.

The pharmaceutical industry's process automation and instrumentation market is also developing fast due to low-cost factors and an evolving regulatory environment. Focus on high-quality equipment indicates progressive buyer maturity and willingness to partner with process control industry players.

Story Continues

Depleting Margins: Material cost inflation, driven by persistent inflationary pressures and rising oil prices, has been adversely impacting industry players’ margins. Transportation costs are also on the rise. Moreover, high raw material prices resulting from continued Middle East tensions and the resulting oil crisis, the prolonged Russia-Ukraine war and the consequent economic sanctions against the Putin regime have affected the production schedules of various firms.

While the companies are focused on improving their operating performances, the inability to obtain adequate supplies of raw materials and product parts at favorable prices is likely to hurt businesses. With firms being unable to pass on the entire increase in raw material prices to customers due to stiff competition, profitability is declining. High customer inventory levels and a conservative approach toward placing orders for high-value items remain headwinds.

Price-sensitive competition for customer retention in the core business is expected to intensify in the coming days. Aggressive competition is likely to limit the ability to attract and retain customers and erode margins. Due to their international footprint, these firms are further exposed to foreign exchange fluctuations that affect their cash flows.

Digitized Technologies at the Core: The industry’s growth is driven mainly by the emphasis on digitized technologies in manufacturing activities, such as the Industrial Internet of Things. The demand for process automation, instrumentation products, safety automation systems and multivariable pressure transmitters for the fast-track manufacturing process is likely to fuel long-term growth opportunities.

The use of process instrumentation equipment offers a host of benefits, including improvement in the quality of the product and emission reduction. Therefore, the rapid adoption of technology across various industries and growing regulation and compliance requirements will continue to be major growth drivers.

In addition, field instruments play a significant role in process control by measuring the key elements, such as temperature, pressure, flow and level in process industries such as chemicals, mining and pharmaceuticals. These include transmitters that measure the pressure, flow, temperature, level and humidity of liquids and gases, which are essential for achieving optimal productivity. A differentiated product offering gives greater opportunities to companies to strengthen their market positions.

Zacks Industry Rank Indicates Bullish Trends

The Zacks Instruments – Control industry is housed within the broader Zacks Computer and Technology sector. It currently has a Zacks Industry Rank #98, which places it in the top 40% of more than 250 Zacks industries.

The group’s Zacks Industry Rank, which is the average of the Zacks Rank of all the member stocks, indicates bright near-term prospects. Our research shows that the top 50% of the Zacks-ranked industries outperform the bottom 50% by a factor of more than 2 to 1.

Before we present a couple of Instruments – Control stocks that you may want to consider for your portfolio, let us take a look at the industry’s recent stock market performance and valuation picture.

Industry Lags S&P 500, Sector

The Zacks Instruments – Control industry has lagged the S&P 500 composite and the broader Zacks Computer and Technology sector over the past year.

The industry has gained 11.9% compared with the S&P 500 and the sector’s growth of 18.1% and 29.8%, respectively.

Industry's Current Valuation

The Enterprise Value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) ratio is commonly used for valuing Instruments – Control stocks. The industry has a trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 8.71X compared with the S&P 500’s 17.17X. It is also below the sector’s trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 17.36X.

Over the past five years, the industry has traded as high as 14.31X and as low as 7.43X, with a median of 9.31X.

2 Instruments Control Stocks to Watch

Sensata: Headquartered in Attleboro, MA, Sensata is a global industrial technology company that develops, manufactures and sells innovative sensor-based solutions. It is considered one of the leading suppliers of electrical protection and power management solutions. The company offers a streamlined set of products, which helps eliminate redundant costs and gives greater pricing flexibility. It invests in cutting-edge technology that enables hybrid and electric vehicles to be more efficient, cost-effective, robust and safe.

The company is expanding its electrification ecosystem to facilitate the seamless transition to electric vehicles, aiming to be a leading provider of mission-critical sensor-rich hardware and software solutions. The stock has soared 39.3% in the past year.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for current-year and next-year earnings has been revised upward by 3.7% and 3.2% to 3.65 and $3.90 per share, respectively, since March 2025. It has a long-term earnings growth expectation of 9.3% and delivered an earnings surprise of 4.7%, on average, in the trailing four quarters. It has a VGM Score of B. Sensata carries a Zacks Rank #3 (Hold). You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank (Strong Buy) stocks here.

Thermon: Headquartered in Austin, TX, Thermon provides engineered industrial process heating solutions for process industries worldwide. Its products include air heaters, boilers, controlling and monitoring solutions, heat tracing systems, tank heating systems, thermostats, tubing bundles, enclosure and explosion-proof gas catalytic heaters, gas-fired blowers and gas heating accessories that comprise regulators, valves, mounting brackets and battery cables.

The buyout of Powerblanket has augmented Thermon's exposure to growing industrial and commercial end markets through its freeze protection, temperature control and flow assurance solutions. It has also enabled the company to expand into adjacent product lines and increase access to diversified end markets. The stock has gained an impressive 78.7% in the past year.

title: 2 Instruments Stocks Set to Ride on Energy-Efficient Industrial Thrust
content:
The Zacks Instruments – Control industry is expected to benefit from energy-efficient production processes and integrated software systems. The rising demand for state-of-the-art technology to replace legacy industrial controls with automated products amid the worldwide energy crisis is likely to support growth.

However, elevated customer inventory levels, high infrastructure upgrade costs, price volatility and inflationary pressures may hinder the process automation and instrumentation market. Amid this backdrop, Sensata Technologies Holdingplc ST and Thermon Group Holdings, Inc. THR are likely to benefit from greater emphasis on energy efficiency, cost-cutting measures and a broad-based thrust on industrial automation and optimal resource utilization.

Industry Description

The Zacks Instruments - Control industry comprises manufacturers of precision and specialty motion-control components and systems used in a wide range of industries. These companies deliver sophisticated flow measurement, control and communication solutions for air, water and other forms of gas and liquid used for commercial and residential purposes. The companies offer an array of products for fuel, combustion, fluid, actuation, electronic applications, energy control and optimization, particularly for the process industry. Some industry players offer heating, ventilation and air conditioning products. These include water heaters and electric heating systems for under-floor radiant applications for boiler manufacturers and alternative energy control packages. A few firms provide water reuse products, including drainage and rainwater harvesting solutions.

What's Shaping the Future of Instruments - Control Industry?

Greater Emphasis on Industrial Automation: A greater focus on increased adoption of automation across all industry verticals and higher investments in new technologies are expected to drive growth over the next few years, with North America leading the way. Rising infrastructural investments in the energy and power sector, increasing demand for organic food and nutritional beverages and favorable government policies are aiding growth. The pharmaceutical industry's process automation and instrumentation market is also developing fast due to low-cost factors and an evolving regulatory environment. Focus on high-quality equipment indicates progressive buyer maturity and willingness to partner with process control industry players. 

Depleting Margins: Material cost inflation, driven by persistent inflationary pressures and rising oil prices, has been adversely impacting industry players’ margins. Transportation costs are also on the rise. Moreover, high raw material prices resulting from continued Middle East tensions and the resulting oil crisis, the prolonged Russia-Ukraine war and the consequent economic sanctions against the Putin regime have affected the production schedules of various firms. While the companies are focused on improving their operating performances, the inability to obtain adequate supplies of raw materials and product parts at favorable prices is likely to hurt businesses. With firms being unable to pass on the entire increase in raw material prices to customers due to stiff competition, profitability is declining. High customer inventory levels and a conservative approach toward placing orders for high-value items remain headwinds. Price-sensitive competition for customer retention in the core business is expected to intensify in the coming days. Aggressive competition is likely to limit the ability to attract and retain customers and erode margins. Due to their international footprint, these firms are further exposed to foreign exchange fluctuations that affect their cash flows.
 
Digitized Technologies at the Core: The industry’s growth is driven mainly by the emphasis on digitized technologies in manufacturing activities, such as the Industrial Internet of Things. The demand for process automation, instrumentation products, safety automation systems and multivariable pressure transmitters for the fast-track manufacturing process is likely to fuel long-term growth opportunities. The use of process instrumentation equipment offers a host of benefits, including improvement in the quality of the product and emission reduction. Therefore, the rapid adoption of technology across various industries and growing regulation and compliance requirements will continue to be major growth drivers. In addition, field instruments play a significant role in process control by measuring the key elements, such as temperature, pressure, flow and level in process industries such as chemicals, mining and pharmaceuticals. These include transmitters that measure the pressure, flow, temperature, level and humidity of liquids and gases, which are essential for achieving optimal productivity. A differentiated product offering gives greater opportunities to companies to strengthen their market positions.

Story Continues

Zacks Industry Rank Indicates Bullish Trends

The Zacks Instruments – Control industry is housed within the broader Zacks Computer and Technology sector. It currently has a Zacks Industry Rank #98, which places it in the top 40% of more than 250 Zacks industries.

The group’s Zacks Industry Rank, which is the average of the Zacks Rank of all the member stocks, indicates bright near-term prospects. Our research shows that the top 50% of the Zacks-ranked industries outperform the bottom 50% by a factor of more than 2 to 1.

Before we present a couple of Instruments – Control stocks that you may want to consider for your portfolio, let us take a look at the industry’s recent stock market performance and valuation picture.

Industry Lags S&P 500, Sector

The Zacks Instruments – Control industry has lagged the S&P 500 composite and the broader Zacks Computer and Technology sector over the past year.

The industry has gained 11.9% compared with the S&P 500 and the sector’s growth of 18.1% and 29.8%, respectively. 

One-Year Price Performance

Industry's Current Valuation

The Enterprise Value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) ratio is commonly used for valuing Instruments – Control stocks. The industry has a trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 8.71X compared with the S&P 500’s 17.17X. It is also below the sector’s trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 17.36X.

Over the past five years, the industry has traded as high as 14.31X and as low as 7.43X, with a median of 9.31X, as the chart below shows.

Trailing 12-Month Enterprise Value-to EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) Ratio

Sensata: Headquartered in Attleboro, MA, Sensata is a global industrial technology company that develops, manufactures and sells innovative sensor-based solutions. It is considered one of the leading suppliers of electrical protection and power management solutions. The company offers a streamlined set of products, which helps eliminate redundant costs and gives greater pricing flexibility. It invests in cutting-edge technology that enables hybrid and electric vehicles to be more efficient, cost-effective, robust and safe. The company is expanding its electrification ecosystem to facilitate the seamless transition to electric vehicles, aiming to be a leading provider of mission-critical sensor-rich hardware and software solutions. The stock has soared 39.3% in the past year. The Zacks Consensus Estimate for current-year and next-year earnings has been revised upward by 3.7% and 3.2% to 3.65 and $3.90 per share, respectively, since March 2025. Its products include air heaters, boilers, controlling and monitoring solutions, heat tracing systems, tank heating systems, thermostats, tubing bundles, enclosure and explosion-proof gas catalytic heaters, gas-fired blowers and gas heating accessories that comprise regulators, valves, mounting brackets and battery cables. The buyout of Powerblanket has augmented Thermon's exposure to growing industrial and commercial end markets through its freeze protection, temperature control and flow assurance solutions. title: Q4 Earnings Highlights: Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) Vs The Rest Of The Analog Semiconductors Stocks
content:
Looking back on analog semiconductors stocks’ Q4 earnings, we examine this quarter’s best and worst performers, including Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) and its peers.

Demand for analog chips is generally linked to the overall level of economic growth, as analog chips serve as the building blocks of most electronic goods and equipment. Unlike digital chip designers, analog chip makers tend to produce the majority of their own chips, as analog chip production does not require expensive leading edge nodes. Less dependent on major secular growth drivers, analog product cycles are much longer, often 5-7 years.

The 15 analog semiconductors stocks we track reported a satisfactory Q4. As a group, revenues beat analysts’ consensus estimates by 0.7% while next quarter’s revenue guidance was above.

Amidst this news, share prices of the companies have had a rough stretch. On average, they are down 9.1% since the latest earnings results.

Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP)

Spun out from General Instrument in 1987, Microchip Technology (NASDAQ: MCHP) is a leading provider of microcontrollers and integrated circuits used mainly in the automotive world, especially in electric vehicles and their charging devices.

Microchip Technology reported revenues of $1.19 billion, up 15.6% year on year. This print exceeded analysts’ expectations by 0.6%. Overall, it was a strong quarter for the company with a solid beat of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates and a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates.

Steve Sanghi, Microchip’s CEO and President commented that, “Our fiscal third quarter results exceeded our expectations, with net sales of $1.186 billion growing 4% sequentially, and 15.6% year-over-year, well above our original guidance. We believe the broad-based recovery across our end markets, combined with significant margin expansion, demonstrates the tangible impact of our nine-point recovery plan execution. Our non-GAAP operating profit grew sequentially more than our net sales did in the December quarter, highlighting the operational momentum we have in our business.”Microchip Technology Total Revenue

Unsurprisingly, the stock is down 18.4% since reporting and currently trades at $63.71.

Is now the time to buy Microchip Technology? Access our full analysis of the earnings results here, it’s free.

Best Q4: Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ:SWKS)

Result of a merger of Alpha Industries and the wireless communications division of Conexant, Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ: SWKS) is a designer and manufacturer of chips used in smartphones, autos, and industrial applications to amplify, filter, and process wireless signals.

Story Continues

Skyworks Solutions reported revenues of $1.04 billion, down 3.1% year on year, outperforming analysts’ expectations by 3.4%. The business had an exceptional quarter with a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates and an impressive beat of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates.Skyworks Solutions Total Revenue

Skyworks Solutions scored the biggest analyst estimates beat among its peers. Although it had a fine quarter compared its peers, the market seems unhappy with the results as the stock is down 1.9% since reporting. It currently trades at $54.85.

Is now the time to buy Skyworks Solutions? Access our full analysis of the earnings results here, it’s free.

Weakest Q4: Vishay Intertechnology (NYSE:VSH)

Named after the founder's ancestral village in present-day Lithuania, Vishay Intertechnology (NYSE:VSH) manufactures simple chips and electronic components that are building blocks of virtually all types of electronic devices.

Vishay Intertechnology reported revenues of $800.9 million, up 12.1% year on year, exceeding analysts’ expectations by 0.7%. Still, it was a slower quarter as it posted a significant miss of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates and EPS in line with analysts’ estimates.

As expected, the stock is down 19.7% since the results and currently trades at $16.66.

Read our full analysis of Vishay Intertechnology’s results here.

Sensata Technologies (NYSE:ST)

Originally a temperature sensor control maker and a subsidiary of Texas Instruments for 60 years, Sensata Technology Holdings (NYSE: ST) is a leading supplier of analog sensors used in industrial and transportation applications, best known for its dominant position in the tire pressure monitoring systems in cars.

Sensata Technologies reported revenues of $917.9 million, up 1.1% year on year. This result beat analysts’ expectations by 0.6%. Taking a step back, it was a mixed quarter as it also recorded a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates but revenue guidance for next quarter meeting analysts’ expectations.

The stock is down 6.9% since reporting and currently trades at $33.68.

Read our full, actionable report on Sensata Technologies here, it’s free.

Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI)

Founded by two MIT graduates, Ray Stata and Matthew Lorber in 1965, Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI) is one of the largest providers of high performance analog integrated circuits used mainly in industrial end markets, along with communications, autos, and consumer devices.

Analog Devices reported revenues of $3.16 billion, up 30.4% year on year. This print surpassed analysts’ expectations by 1.4%. It was a very strong quarter as it also produced revenue guidance for next quarter exceeding analysts’ expectations and a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates.

Analog Devices pulled off the fastest revenue growth among its peers. The stock is down 7.5% since reporting and currently trades at $312.30.

Read our full, actionable report on Analog Devices here, it’s free.

Market Update

Late in 2025 into early 2026, there was hand wringing around artificial intelligence. For software companies, the fear was that AI would erode pricing power and compress margins as new tools made it easier to replicate what once required expensive enterprise platforms. Crypto investors had their own version of the same anxiety: if AI agents could trade, allocate capital, and manage wallets autonomously, what exactly was the long-term value of today’s crypto infrastructure?

These concerns triggered a noticeable rotation away from these sectors and into safer havens. But markets rarely dwell on one narrative for long. Spring 2026 came, and the focus shifted abruptly from technological disruption to geopolitical risk. The US’ conflict with Iran became the dominant driver of market psychology, and when geopolitics takes center stage, the script changes quickly. Investors stop debating growth rates and start worrying about oil supply, inflation, and global stability.

title: Sensata Technologies: Underwriting Industrial Stability in a Slowing Auto Cycle
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Introduction

Sensata Technologies sits in an uncomfortable category in today's market. It is being valued primarily as a cyclical automotive supplier at a time when global auto production is uneven and electrification trends remain uncertain. Yet the underlying economics of the business point to something more stable: a company that continues to generate substantial free cash flow, maintain solid operating margins, and steadily reduce leverage. The gap between how the market is framing the business and how the cash flow actually behaves is where the investment case begins.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 6 Warning Signs with ST. Is ST fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

For the year ended Dec. 31, 2025, the company reported revenue of $3.7045 billion, down 5.8% on a reported basis, driven primarily by divestitures and product lifecycle actions; on an organic basis, revenue was roughly flat year over year. Adjusted operating income was $704.9 million, a 19.0% margin, modestly lower than the prior year, with tariff pass-through revenue cited as a small drag on margin. Free cash flow was $490.2 million on net cash from operating activities of $621.5 million, and the company ended the year with $573.0 million of cash on hand. Sensata returned $191.0 million to shareholders across dividends and repurchases, while also strengthening its balance sheet: net debt ended 2025 at $2.294 billion, down $336 million from the prior year.

The latest earnings print tightened the near-term picture and is worth acknowledging explicitly. In the fourth quarter, revenue was $917.9 million, up 1.1% year over year (organic growth of 3.5%), and adjusted operating income was $179.7 million, a 19.6% margin, modestly above the prior-year quarter. Free cash flow in the quarter was $151.8 million, which management described as 117% conversion. In other words, the quarter did not require ideal end-market conditions to produce cash, and it reinforced the idea that deleveraging remains a tangible driver of owner value even if the top line stays muted.

Structurally, Sensata's reorganization into Automotive, Industrials, and Aerospace, Defense & Commercial Equipment is a reasonable reflection of how the company actually lives through cycles. Automotive remains the largest segment, but the other two segments can temper the amplitude of the vehicle cycle and create pockets of growth tied to automation, energy infrastructure, and aerospace modernization. Management's first-quarter 2026 outlook, revenue of $917 million to $937 million (inclusive of tariff cost recovery) and adjusted EPS of $0.81 to $0.85, frames the base case: modest growth, stable margins, and continued focus on cash conversion rather than a near-term acceleration story.

Story Continues

The Economic Engine

Sensata's economics are anchored in small components that sit in critical positions. A pressure sensor that fails can shut down a braking system. A contactor that misfires can disable a battery pack. That positioning matters more than brand recognition. The company's products are typically low-cost relative to the system they protect, but high consequence in failure. That dynamic supports pricing stability and embeds the company in OEM platforms for multi-year vehicle cycles.

In 2025, Sensata generated $3.704 billion in revenue. Automotive remained the largest segment, accounting for approximately 56% of total revenue, while Industrials represented roughly 33% and Aerospace, Defense & Commercial Equipment contributed the remaining portion. The Automotive segment declined year over year in line with global light vehicle production softness and portfolio pruning actions. Industrial revenue was more stable, supported by gas leak detection and electrification-related applications. Aerospace and defense delivered growth in selected subsegments despite broader weakness in commercial truck end markets.

Adjusted operating income for 2025 was $704.9 million, equating to a 19.0% adjusted operating margin. This compares to margins above 20% in prior cycles, indicating modest compression but not structural impairment. The company highlighted tariff-related impacts and product lifecycle actions as headwinds, partially offset by productivity initiatives and pricing adjustments.

Cash conversion remains one of the company's more durable characteristics. In 2025, Sensata produced $621.5 million of operating cash flow and $490.2 million of free cash flow. That implies free cash flow conversion of roughly 70% of adjusted operating income, a healthy ratio for a business exposed to cyclical end markets. Working capital discipline remains critical here. The business operates with meaningful inventory and receivables tied to OEM production schedules, yet it continues to generate consistent operating cash even in a softer volume environment.

The structural debate around Sensata centers on electrification. Electric vehicles typically require fewer traditional combustion-related sensors but more battery management, thermal management, and high-voltage protection components. Sensata has repositioned its portfolio toward high-voltage contactors and electrification-adjacent content. The question for owners is whether the mix shift supports margin stability or introduces new pricing pressure as OEMs rationalize EV costs.

Industrial exposure provides diversification, but it is not immune to macro forces. Industrial automation, energy infrastructure, and gas detection provide structural tailwinds, yet industrial demand can weaken quickly in a capital expenditure slowdown. Sensata's diversification moderates volatility but does not eliminate cyclicality.

The engine, then, is neither a pure growth story nor a fragile cyclical supplier. It is a high-margin component franchise embedded in complex systems, operating across end markets that move at different speeds. The durability of that engine depends less on headline vehicle production and more on whether content per platform and electrification exposure can offset volume variability.Sensata Technologies: Underwriting Industrial Stability in a Slowing Auto Cycle

Capital Structure and Capital Allocation

Sensata's equity story only makes sense once the balance sheet is treated as the center of gravity. This is not a business where a modest miss on end-market demand simply trims the quarterly number. With leverage, the question becomes whether the company can keep converting earnings into cash while steadily shrinking net debt, because that is what ultimately expands the owner's claim on the enterprise.

At the end of 2025, Sensata reported total gross debt of $2.867 billion and cash and cash equivalents of $573 million, implying net debt of $2.294 billion. On the company's definition, net leverage ended the year at 2.7x last-twelve-month adjusted EBITDA. The key detail is not the ratio alone, but the direction: net debt declined by roughly $336 million versus year-end 2024. In other words, 2025 was not a year of maintaining leverage; it was a year of deliberate balance sheet tightening.

That tightening was funded by cash generation, not financial engineering. Operating cash flow was $621.5 million in 2025 and free cash flow was $490.2 million, with the company reporting a 97% free cash flow conversion rate relative to adjusted net income. That level of conversion matters because it gives management real optionality: debt reduction is not dependent on an external refinancing window, and it does not require cutting investment to the bone.

The way Sensata split that cash is also telling because the outcomes are measurable, not theoretical. In 2025, the company returned $191.0 million to shareholders, including $70.4 million of dividends and $120.6 million of share repurchases. In 2024, it returned another $141.1 million, including $72.2 million of dividends and $68.9 million of buybacks. Over the same two-year period, net debt declined from $2.92 billion at the end of 2023 to $2.63 billion at the end of 2024 and then to $2.29 billion at the end of 2025. That matters because it shows management was not using the balance sheet to preserve the appearance of discipline. It was reducing leverage while still returning capital. The financing cash flows in 2025 also make clear that this was an active liability-management year rather than a simple pay down what comes due year: the company repaid $352.2 million of debt, partially offset by $500 million of new borrowings, while still shrinking net debt materially. For an owner, that is the right combination of signals. The business is generating enough cash to support shareholder returns, but management is still improving the balance sheet in ways that lower the equity's sensitivity to a weak demand environment.

There is a second, less comfortable layer to capital allocation that matters for underwriting: the business has been actively pruning and restructuring, and those actions have not been costless. The 2025 results included material restructuring-related and other charges, and a non-cash goodwill impairment tied to the Dynapower business. Management also disclosed charges connected to policy and regulatory shifts in clean energy and excess capacity around electrification, including impacts from an electric vehicle program cancellation by a customer. This is the kind of detail that often gets waved away as non-recurring, but as an owner it deserves attention because it reveals where the portfolio has been mis-positioned and where capital has not earned its keep.

The allocation framework going forward therefore has a clear hierarchy. First, the company needs to preserve the cash conversion profile that allows net debt to keep stepping down through a cycle. Second, share repurchases only make sense when they do not stall deleveraging. Third, growth investment must be judged by incremental return rather than by narrative, particularly in electrification-adjacent categories where demand can be real but pricing and utilization can still disappoint.

This is the core capital question Sensata presents. The business can look stable on the income statement even in a soft automotive environment, but the owner outcome is shaped more by how management compresses net debt and avoids repeating the kinds of mis-steps that produced impairments and restructuring in 2025.

Valuation

Sensata is not priced like a compounder. It is priced like a business the market expects to behave through a down auto cycle, but not necessarily improve through it. That framing is visible in the enterprise math more than the earnings multiple.

On current market values, Sensata's equity is roughly a $5.15 billion claim on an enterprise value of about $7.50 billion. Against that, the company generated $490.2 million of free cash flow in 2025. Even before making any heroic assumptions about growth, that puts the equity free cash flow yield at roughly 9.5%. The owner question is straightforward: if the business can hold something close to that cash generation through a soft production environment, the equity does not need multiple expansion to produce a reasonable return. It needs durability, and it needs continued balance sheet compression.

A second way to see the same point is to start at the enterprise level. On trailing EBITDA, Sensata now sits at roughly 15.0x EV/EBITDA. That is neither distressed nor premium. It makes sense: the company is not structurally broken, but it does not carry the balance sheet or end-market visibility that earns the high-quality sensor leaders their multiples. What matters, though, is that Sensata has been converting profit into cash and using that cash to reduce net leverage. If free cash flow remains in the $450$500 million range and management prioritizes debt reduction, owners are underwriting a return that is driven primarily by two levers: the cash yield they receive today and the incremental equity value created as net debt steps down.

A conservative underwriting frame looks like this. Assume no multiple expansion and assume free cash flow simply holds roughly flat in nominal terms across a cycle. If the equity is purchased at a high-single-digit free cash flow yield and the company uses a meaningful portion of that cash to shrink net debt, the owner's return can plausibly land in the low-double-digits without relying on a rerating. The risk, of course, is that end-market pressure and mix shift reduce the cash flow base at the same time the balance sheet is still being digested. That is why the multiple looks reasonable rather than cheap. The market is explicitly charging the equity for cyclicality and leverage.

Company TTM P/E EV/EBITDA Operating Margin ROIC Net Debt / EBITDA Sensata Technologies 173.6x 15.0x 13.0% 8.6% ~4.9x Aptiv 96.6x 10.9x 11.7% 9.4% 2.3x BorgWarner 51.7x 9.5x 10.4% 10.1% 1.6x TE Connectivity 30.6x 16.0x 19.8% 15.0% 1.1x Amphenol 38.4x 25.6x 27.5% 27.9% 1.9x

This comparison is not about identifying the lowest multiple. It is about understanding why multiples differ. Amphenol trades at roughly 2526x EV/EBITDA because it consistently earns high-20s operating margins and high-20s ROIC while maintaining moderate leverage. TE Connectivity, with mid-teens ROIC and near-20% operating margins, also commands a structurally higher multiple than auto-heavy suppliers. In both cases, valuation is supported by demonstrably stronger capital efficiency and balance sheet flexibility.

Sensata sits in a different position. Its EV/EBITDA multiple of roughly 15x is closer to the automotive supplier complex, and the economic data explains why. Operating margins are materially lower than TE and Amphenol, ROIC is in the high single digits, and net leverage remains meaningfully higher. That gap in capital efficiency and balance sheet strength justifies some discount.

The relevant question, then, is not whether Sensata should trade like Amphenol. It is whether the current valuation already discounts its structural weaknesses more than its cash durability warrants. If margins stabilize and free cash flow continues to reduce leverage, the multiple does not need to expand for owners to earn a satisfactory return. The discount only becomes punitive if earnings erode faster than debt declines.

What ultimately determines the owner outcome at today's price is not whether the multiple expands, but whether the current cash yield persists. With free cash flow of $490.2 million in 2025 and a market capitalization that implies an equity free cash flow yield of roughly 9.5%, the arithmetic is straightforward. Even assuming flat free cash flow and no multiple expansion, continued net debt reduction in the $300$400 million annual range would incrementally increase equity value through balance sheet compression alone. Under that framework, the base case return is driven by cash generation and deleveraging rather than rerating.

Valuable Investors

What stands out in the disclosed ownership is not a single star position but the mix of investor types clustering around the stock at the same time. This is not the profile of a crowded momentum name. It is a shareholder base that appears comfortable underwriting cyclical pressure so long as the cash engine remains intact and leverage trends down.

The most meaningful signal in the chart is the direction of change. Several valuation-driven managers are adding aggressively into the weakness rather than trimming. Pzena Investment Management increased its position by 380.41% to 3.5 million shares ($117 million), and Barrow Hanley increased by 28.68% to 4.3 million shares ($144 million). Heartland Advisors also increased by 59.8%. Those are not token moves. They suggest the stock is being treated as a balance-sheet-and-cycle underwriting case, not a wait for perfect conditions story.

On the other side, the chart also shows what looks like selective caution rather than a broad exit. Olstein Capital reduced the position by 17.92%, while Elliott Investment Management held steady at 3.3 million shares ($108 million). The combination matters: there is evidence of rotation among value managers, but not a collapse in sponsorship. Elliott's unchanged stake is particularly relevant in a business where capital allocation discipline and portfolio decisions can drive per-share outcomes; even without inferring intent, the presence of a large, steady holder reinforces that the debate is being framed around owner economics rather than near-term sentiment.

Finally, the base is not purely discretionary. Dimensional Fund Advisors holds 7.3 million shares ($242 million) and increased modestly, which is consistent with the stock screening as a value/cash flow candidate within systematic portfolios. Point72's increase and Gotham's smaller add reinforce that the stock is not owned only by one tribe.

Taken together, the chart supports a simple interpretation: the shareholder base looks prepared to live through volatility if cash conversion holds and leverage continues to decline. That aligns with the central underwriting question in this article, and it is a more useful takeaway than reciting every line item.

Structural Risks

The first risk is that Sensata's end markets can weaken in ways that are not cleanly reversible, and the income statement can look stable while the mix is deteriorating. Automotive remains the largest exposure, and when global light vehicle production softens, the company can protect margins for a period through cost actions, pricing and portfolio pruning. The problem is that prolonged softness tends to show up later in the form of program churn and under-absorption. Sensata disclosed that an electric vehicle program cancellation by a customer contributed to restructuring-related and other charges in the fourth quarter of 2025. That is a useful signal for owners: electrification content is not a straight-line tailwind, and customer decisions can strand capacity or engineering effort faster than the market assumes.

The second risk is that electrification has become a policy-linked market rather than a purely technological one. In 2025, Sensata recorded a non-cash goodwill impairment charge related to Dynapower and other charges tied to changes in clean energy policy, emissions regulations, and excess capacity related to electrification. Those are not accounting curiosities. They are evidence that the company has already deployed capital into parts of the electrification value chain where demand, incentives, and utilization can shift abruptly. If policy support weakens or OEMs slow electrification investment to protect near-term profitability, Sensata can face another round of portfolio reshaping that consumes cash and management attention.

The third risk is that trade and tariff exposure remains a persistent tax on margins. Sensata has discussed tariff pass-through revenue as dilutive to adjusted operating income margin in both the quarter and the full year, which implies that even when pricing mechanisms exist, the timing and friction of recovery can still compress profitability. Owners should treat this as a structural earnings-quality risk: a company can report steady revenue while the economics of that revenue are being quietly pressured by cost pass-through mechanics and customer resistance.

The final risk is operational resilience. Sensata disclosed a ransomware incident that encrypted certain servers and temporarily impacted operations including shipping, receiving, and manufacturing production. Management can reasonably argue these events are containable, but for an owner, the relevant point is that this is a high-volume component supplier with customers that run tight production schedules. Disruptions that slow shipments or compromise systems can become customer relationship issues, not just IT cleanup costs. The incident also introduces regulatory, legal, and remediation cost uncertainty that is difficult to underwrite cleanly from the outside.

Conclusion

Sensata does not require a rerating to work. At roughly a high-single-digit free cash flow yield and with net debt already reduced to $2.29 billion in 2025, the return profile can be driven by arithmetic rather than sentiment. If free cash flow remains in the $450500 million range and management continues prioritizing debt reduction, incremental equity value is created simply through balance sheet compression. That is a permanent-owner case, not a timing trade.

The discount to higher-quality peers is not irrational. Operating margins and ROIC trail TE Connectivity and Amphenol, and leverage remains meaningfully higher. Some gap is justified. The question is whether the current valuation already embeds those structural differences in full.

For a long-term owner, a lower multiple is not the primary danger. The real risk is erosion of the cash base itself. If program volatility, electrification misallocation, tariff friction, or operational disruption structurally reduce earnings power, then deleveraging becomes defensive rather than accretive. In that scenario, the equity can look optically cheap while intrinsic value declines.

But if the recent earnings trend, stable margins, continued cash conversion, and steady net debt reduction, persists, the stock does not need to trade at 20 EBITDA to generate a reasonable outcome. It needs earnings durability and discipline. Under those conditions, today's valuation offers a return driven by cash yield and leverage normalization rather than optimism about a multiple expansion.

That is a narrower thesis than a rerating argument. It is also more controllable.

title: DigiKey Collaborates with STMicroelectronics and Ultra Librarian to Deliver Enhanced eDesignSuite Integration
content:
Free, browser-based design environment streamlines engineering workflows from simulation to BOM export and purchasing

THIEF RIVER FALLS, Minn., March 19, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- DigiKey, the global distribution leader of electronic components and automation products, announces the availability of an enhanced eDesignSuite experience, developed through a collaboration with STMicroelectronics (ST) and Ultra Librarian. The improved tool environment allows engineers to design, simulate, refine and confidently validate their projects using ST components and to purchase their entire bill of materials (BOM) directly from DigiKey in a few easy steps.The enhanced eDesignSuite from DigiKey, STMicroelectronics and Ultra Librarian helps engineers design, simulate and validate projects with integrated BOM refinement and multi-CAD export capabilities.

The eDesignSuite, developed by ST, is a free, publicly accessible, no-license-required online design platform that provides a unified workflow for power management, signal conditioning, NFC/RFID applications and other design domains. The collaboration with DigiKey and Ultra Librarian expands the platform's capabilities by tightly integrating component models, simulation flows, BOM management and multi-CAD exports.

"DigiKey is excited to collaborate with ST and Ultra Librarian to bring engineers a unified online tool that accelerates their design process," said Nick D. Olson, supplier business development manager at DigiKey. "The deep integration within the eDesignSuite environment gives users advanced BOM verification, enhanced simulation insight, and a direct path from design to purchase, all of which help bring new products to market faster."

The enhanced eDesignSuite experience provides several new and expanded capabilities, including:

Integrated thermal and electrical simulation support through SIMPLIS/SIMetrix, enabling deeper performance verification and design confidence Real-time BOM refinement with live impact analysis, allowing engineers to immediately see how parameter changes affect design choices and performance Seamless export of schematics and BOMs to multiple CAD environments, including OrCAD, Altium and Eagle Application-specific design support, including power management design, thermal-electrical simulation, signal conditioning and NFC/RFID calculators

These capabilities make ST the first integrated circuit vendor to offer this level of design tool connectivity with DigiKey's purchasing systems.

The enhanced eDesignSuite provides an end-to-end workflow that helps engineers:

Efficiently evaluate operating conditions, waveforms and design alternatives Validate the impact of component selections Generate finalized schematics and BOM files Export to preferred CAD environments Purchase the entire BOM from DigiKey in a few streamlined steps

Story Continues

"This valuable collaboration with DigiKey and Ultra Librarian extends eDesignSuite from a powerful design tool into a unified design-to-purchase environment," said Patrizia Bellitto, SW system tools - appl. manager at STMicroelectronics. "By connecting simulation, component selection, CAD export and purchasing in a single browser-based environment, we're simplifying the engineer's journey and making it easier to design with ST technologies."

"Ultra Librarian is focused on simplifying the path from design to procurement," said Gopu Achath, vice president of technology at Ultra Librarian. "Through our integration with STMicroelectronics eDesignSuite, engineers can now seamlessly access component data and upload BOMs to DigiKey, creating a faster and more connected design environment."

The eDesignSuite is publicly available and free to use at ST.com. For more information about DigiKey's suite of design tools, visit the DigiKey website.

About DigiKey DigiKey, headquartered in Thief River Falls, Minn., USA, is the global distribution leader of electronic components and automation products. DigiKey delivers the broadest and deepest selection of products available for immediate shipment worldwide, offering more than 17.5 million components from nearly 3,000 trusted manufacturers. Engineers, designers, builders and procurement professionals rely on DigiKey for innovative digital solutions, design tools and frictionless purchasing experiences that simplify every stage of their projects. Discover more about DigiKey's electronics distribution, automation solutions and design resources at digikey.com and on Facebook, X, YouTube, Instagram and LinkedIn.

About STMicroelectronics At ST, we are 48,000 creators and makers of semiconductor technologies mastering the semiconductor supply chain with state-of-the-art manufacturing facilities. An integrated device manufacturer, we work with more than 200,000 customers and thousands of partners to design and build products, solutions, and ecosystems that address their challenges and opportunities, and the need to support a more sustainable world. Our technologies enable smarter mobility, more efficient power and energy management, and the wide-scale deployment of cloud-connected autonomous things. We are on track to be carbon neutral in all direct and indirect emissions (scopes 1 and 2), product transportation, business travel, and employee commuting emissions (our scope 3 focus), and to achieve our 100% renewable electricity sourcing goal by the end of 2027. Further information can be found at www.st.com.

About Ultra Librarian
Ultra Librarian is the world's largest free CAD library, providing millions of verified symbols, footprints, and 3D models to streamline the electronic design process. Trusted by engineers worldwide, Ultra Librarian helps reduce design time, improve accuracy, and bring products to market faster. www.ultralibrarian.com.
